sentences.text: No, excuse me.
It-- they-- it wasn't occupied yet.
The Belgians arrested him because he had German papers.
We were-- had-- we were-- had a German passport still.
And he was arrested as a German.
And so was this Werner.
And they wanted him out because they figured that they are the Fifth Column and that they will spy for Germany.
No, it was not--
The bombs were already falling, though.
Yeah, well, the bombs-- that was very short that, that-- the-- there, there wasn't too much damage that--
Yeah, the Belgian police arrested the German men because they were afraid of espionage.
And they put them on the trains and sent them to France.
And all we knew was that they're going to send them to France.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I always say, you know, the communication was so bad.
There was no television.
And there was not much radio.
And it was in French.
We only lived there a few months.
We spoke a little French, but not enough, I guess, to understand what's going on.
We lived on the fringes of society, so to speak.
And I pestered my mother.
I want to go to France.
I want to see if we can't find my father, if we can-- I don't know.
I had big fantasies, I guess.
And my mother didn't want to deny me that.
So we set out.
And we went from-- first from Brussels on a train to Antwerp and-- to go to the French border.
So today, that's, I think, a half-hour ride.
And even then, it didn't-- in peacetime, it took-- it didn't take more than an hour by train.
We were a whole day on that train.
And it was bombardment.
And there was glass.
And there was everything.
When we came to Antwerp and had gotten out of this train, it was already night, dark.
We stumbled through thick glass.
I think Antwerp was much more bombarded than Brussels.
And there were thousands of refugees.
And it was just bedlam.
So they said, go this way and go to the cloister-- to, to the convent.
They'll put you up for the night.
So all these refugees went there.
And then down in the basement, in the cellar, we were all sitting, more or less.
And the nuns were running around with candles.
There was no electricity.
They had all  been bombarded out.
And we spent the night there very frightened and very unsure what is going to happen.
So the next morning, we got out on the street.
And again, there was bedlam.
But there-- these trolley cars-- there is a trolley car that goes from, from Antwerp along the coast to the French border.
So there were hundreds of people.
And we took this trolley car, not quite to the border.
But-- and that's the first time I saw the ocean, actually, from there.
I was very terribly impressed.
Well, I had seen the ocean from the ship.
But it's not the same thing.
So we came to a little village and-- along with hundreds of others of refugees.
And we met up with a family.
It was a woman with two children that had been on the with us.
And we stayed together with her.
And we rented a room.
We found a room in the hotel.
And we stayed all together there-- big room.
And they told us the French border is closed.
So we were stuck there.
We couldn't go back.
And we couldn't get forward.
So we stayed the few days.
And then they said, tomorrow morning, the border will be open.
But you have to walk to the border.
It was not, I don't know, a few kilometers.
I don't remember.
So we, we walked to the border.
We just had a little suitcase.
And and my mother had her famous silver fox.
And that was a symbol, I guess, of her former days in Germany.
And she didn't want to give this up.
So instead of taking anything else, she had taken that silver fox.
And we came to the border.
And here again, we had German passports.
So with all these people were running, and pressing, and pushing, we had still had to show the passport to go through.
And when he saw our German passport, he said, go back to Germany!
Go back to Germany!
We don't want you!
You cannot come in, you know.
The German was their attacker.
And when we were the archenemies there.
And so here comes a young man with a bicycle.
And my mother's fox was hanging there.
And the fox was-- got entangled in this bicycle wheel.
And, you know, it went around.
[LAUGHS] [POINTS] And he went over the border with his bicycle.
And my mother was crying, mon renard!
Mon renard!
My fox!
My fox!
And she kept-- grabbed me by the hand.
And I grabbed that lady with her children.
And boom, we were all five over the border with the-- [LAUGHS] with the silver fox.
Well, It wasn't much of a improvement because there were thousands of refugees there.
And they were all walking.
And so we walked along with them on the highway.
And we came-- we slept in the school.
And the schools were-- there, there were some aides.
They made big pots of Jell-O or soup, or something.
I just remember that great big thing that we enter.
Everybody was dipping in and eating.
And we were sleeping and setting up on the floor.
And this other lady was always [LAUGHS] very high strung and funny.
And she, she must have had some money sewed into her coat.
And she was always having her coat on.
And when she woke up, she says, are they here already?
Are they here already?
Well, we knew that the Germans were coming, you know, and there was no escaping.
And so, sure enough, after two or three days, we heard the German planes overhead.
And they were machine-gunning the highways to make room for the soldiers marching, you know.
They didn't-- if they hit, they hit.
They didn't care.
But that was really my worst moment where I was really, really afraid.
Because you are unprotected on a highway-- mostly refugees, no soldiers, no nothing.
And here are the planes.
And they machine-gun you.
So we ran into the nearest house.
We didn't even ask if they let us in.
We just went in.
And maybe two hours later, they marched-- the German troops marched in.
[SIGHS]
So we-- after-- we couldn't stay with these people, you know.
We couldn't stay in this house.
So after they passed through, we started walking again.
And we walked.
And we came to a big estate, like a big, little castle.
And we went in there.
And there was an old woman.
She said, yeah, you can stay at the servant's house.
There was an extra little, like, we would say today, a guest house.
But it was for the servants.
She said, it's empty.
You can stay at the servant's house.
So the five of us piled in there.
We had nothing.
And-- but maybe next day or so, the German High Command made headquarters in that castle because that was the biggest house in the-- in the village.
And they moved in there.
So we were quite afraid what's going-- you know, if they find us or what.
Here again, my mother, the courageous one, she said, well, I think honesty is always the best policy.
I'm going to go over and tell them who we are, and that we are refugees.
And we are there.
So she went over.
And she spoke German.
And she said nothing about religion.
She just said, we are refugees.
And we are two women with three children here.
And we are in this guest house.
And we just want you to know, so you don't accuse us of espionage or who knows what, you know.
And they were very nice.
They sent bread over.
And they sent food over.
And they knew or didn't know who we were.
I don't know.
But we had, you know, they didn't persecute us or anything.
So we stayed and then-- in this house and in this thing for about a week.
It was in the-- in the-- on the northern coast of France, very close to Belgium.
I mean, we walked this whole thing.
So how far could we have gone?
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot Dunkerque.
So anyhow, we [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] stayed there about a week.
And then we decided the best thing is to go back to Brussels somehow.
We cannot stay in those villages.
We have to go on living, you know.
So this-- there was a young boy of about 13, 14 years old.
And he, he somehow found a bicycle.
And we found a, a, you know, a-- how do you call it?
Tschhh-- carriage of yeah, [GERMAN].
And we put everything on there.
And we started walking again.
So we walked to the next town.
And there, they also took in refugees in the-- in the schools.
Usually-- mostly, they opened up the schools, you know, so we could-- the people could sleep there.
And we stayed there a few days.
And we saw no progress and no way of going back.
So then I was the courageous.
And I went to the headquarters, to the Germans.
And I said, we are here.
I am here with my mother, and a friend, and two younger children.
And we want to go back to Brussels.
Is there a way that we can have transportation?
They said, yeah, tomorrow morning, come and there are all these camions, all these trucks going to Brussels.
And we'll take you.
So they wanted me, of course.
I was the young girl.
I was 16 or 17 by this time.
They wanted me in the front with the truck driver and the others in the back.
I said, no, my mother goes, too.
I don't want anybody in the back.
So they went in the-- in another truck in the front.
And of course, he didn't say anything against Jews.
But somehow, in the course of the conversation, I told him that I'm Jewish.
And he wouldn't believe it.
He said, oh, I can't believe it.
There must have been an accident.
I said, there's no accident.
We're Jews from way back.
And, you know, that was 1940.
And, and, in, in the West, they had orders not to do anything.
And when they came to Eastern Europe, they immediately saw the Jews and put them into ghettos, and so forth.
But in the West, they had orders at that time, in 1940, not to do anything.
So somehow, we got back to Brussels.
And, of course, we never saw my father again.
But we started to have communications.
We had relatives in Switzerland.
And he could write to Switzerland.
And they send us the letters.
And then he wrote to me Werner Marcus is in the same barrack with him.
He was in Gurs by this time.
There was Saint Cyprian first.
They had camps.
There were camps where the Spanish prisoner of wars were first housed from the Spanish Civil War.
There were refugees.
And so the camps were already in place when they came.
Then he was in Camp Gurs.
And Gurs was a big camp.
There were women and men, but I think separated.
And we were able to send him packages-- a little-- some packages.
And one day, my mother sent a package.
And she said, oh, I forgot to pack the handkerchiefs.
So she took a handkerchief out of her pocketbook and put it into the package and-- no, to another lady-- another-- a strange lady, she gave that handkerchief to pack up.
And when this man came and got his package, there was a handkerchief embroidered 'Gerda Blachmann.' And he showed this to a young man next to him in the barrack.
He said, isn't it funny?
My wife sent me a handkerchief with the name Gerda Blachmann on it?
And this man was Werner Marcus.
It was just like providence.
Of all the time thousands of people, he said, can I have it?
This is my friend.
I know her.
And this, you know, it was really strange.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mother and I, we lived in Brussels for about two years.
What papers did you have at this point in Brussels?
No papers-- just still the German passport.
I don't think I got-- received letters from Werner, but from my father, yes.
And I have some of those letters.
Not too much because this must have been censored, too, you know.
But he wrote mostly that he was not feeling that well and that he was sick and that he-- I mean, he was very depressed and not very happy.
And my mother's brother, Gerhard Tebel, was also in this camp.
So my mother and I-- we lived in Brussels that time.
We found another apartment.
What we had left there in a hurry-- the landlady had mostly stolen everything.
And there wasn't much left.
But we were able to get that lift that we had packed in Breslau.
Somehow my grandparents paid for it again.
And they sent it again.
So we were able to sell things out of this lift.
And I started, like, an apprenticeship with a dressmaker and made a little bit of money.
And my mother made a little bit of money.
So we were able to, to make-- to live for about two years.
And mother was very friendly with this lady that-- Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH] at the time-- that came back with us.
We were always together.
So-- but things-- the Germans started to tighten the screws already.

So they were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and one day, they wrote in the newspapers, or they had posters on the streets that all the Jews have to register [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not the headquarters, at the city hall.
And here again, my mother was smart.
She said, I'm not going to register.
If they don't know that I exist, they cannot come for me.
I'm not going to register.
And the other lady said, yes, we have to.
We have to.
And they're going to punish us.
And she did.
Most people did.
But my mother insisted she was not going to register.

And sure enough, after about six months or so when the-- they-- people that had registered started to get letters that they should present themselves at this and this time at the train station for work-- to go-- to be sent to work.
And a lot of people did, obviously.
And that was the beginning of the deportations.
They didn't say deportations.
They said, present yourself to work.
And we never got such an letter because they didn't have our address.
And of course, we could have been caught in other ways because they started to make razias and, you know, to go into the houses and look for Jews.
And they closed whole streets on both sides.
And everybody on that street had to present their papers.
Now, we had a German passport.
But we had a big J in it, you know, for Jude, for Jew.
And the same on trolley cars.
They closed up trolley cars on both sides and, and inspected everybody that was on it.
So it wasn't the only way that we could have been caught.
But it was a big thing that we didn't have-- the register.
So one day, this Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH] got her letter with the children.
And the boy came running, Mrs.
Blachmann, Mrs.
Blachmann, help us!
Mother is packing.
She wants us to go to the work, you know, to work.
And my mother ran over and-- over there.
And she didn't live far from us.
And she lived over a little grocery store.
And these people were very nice.
I mean, the Belgian people were very good and helpful on the whole.

You know, there were some not, but-- so she had offered to take the girl, who was about 10, to the country and to have her with her relatives, Belgian relatives, and have her live there in kind of hiding.
So she said, you send [?
Leah ?] to the-- to the country.
And you go.
There was, you know, bed and-- like we have here bed and breakfast.
They have [NON-ENGLISH].
You check into a [NON-ENGLISH] under a different name.
And you stay there.
And we'll see what happens.
And don't you dare go present yourself to go to work.
She did.
She did this.
And so my mother had, in the meantime, found a, a place where she thought we will be hiding.
And we went.
And then we heard that-- one night that they were going to come and search the houses.
So mother and I went into this [NON-ENGLISH], in this little room upstairs and-- to hide.
And I said to my mother, you know-- first of all, we didn't have that much money to be able to pay for the rest of the war.
It was very-- the people took a lot of money for that.
And I said besides, we're going to go crazy.
We can't just the two of us sit here for years and, and be hiding.
So we came out of hiding.
[LAUGHS] And she looked for others.
And she had made some other friends,  a Belgian friend.

And she was able to connect us with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with an Underground [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] organization that really smuggled British prisoners of wars out of Germany through Switzerland and through Portugal and back to England.
And somehow, she made a connection there.
I don't know how.
And they said, OK, you're the first woman that we do this for.
Because there were, like here, the coyotes, you know, like people that took people illegally to Switzerland.
But it was very expensive.
You know, they took thousands of dollars, or franks, or whatever.
And we didn't have that much money.
She-- they gave us false papers like were born in-- we would have been born in Alsace-Lorraine, you know, in, Alsace-- what is it in English?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The border place between France and Germany.
So they-- these people didn't speak French so perfectly.
They had, like, a German accent like we had.
So it would have been more plausible if we would have been caught that we didn't-- we spoke French by then, but not perfectly and certainly not without an accent.
So we had these false papers.
And we were preparing to leave, like, on a Sunday.
And the Wednesday before, we got a postcard from my father from Drancy that he's being deported.
And in the meantime, Petain had come to power.
And the south of France was no, no longer free.
It was under Petain.
And Petain took all the Jews out of those camps.
And the German Germans--- non-Jewish Germans he took back to Germany.
And the Jewish Germans they took all to Drancy and-- for deportation.
I even have the list, the-- what the-- Holocaust Museum in Washington sent me the list, the deportation list out of Drancy.
And my father's name is on it right at  the beginning, with his birth date and everything.
So he was not in the best condition already-- you know, two years in the French camps already.
And he, he went.
And so did my uncle, apparently.
But Werner was young.
He was 18.
And he had been liberated already by ORT, you know, the ORT organization was big in France.
And he was not quite 18.
So he, he qualified as a child yet.
And he was in an ORT school in France.
And when the deportations started, the, the mayor of this little town came to the school and said, send-- tomorrow morning, the SS will come and get all the children.
Send the bigger boys and girls.
Give them whatever you can.
And send them out.
They have to fend for themselves.
But they have a chance-- better the chance than sitting here and being picked up tomorrow morning.
So he went.
And he spoke very well, French.
He was very gifted for languages.
And in the meantime, he spoke a very good French.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow, through months of walking during the night and sleeping in the fields during the day and working a little bit here and there in the hotels and on farms, he made his way to Switzerland.
And he, he came into Switzerland.
He was already there.
So-- and we had-- well, I had the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so mother-- here again, we didn't have too much money.
So somehow, we got together with another Belgian Jewish woman who had three children.
And she needed help with the children.
So they said, if you'll take care of her and help with the children, she will pay all the expenses for you.
We, we had-- we left with 10 marks out of Germany.
So we got together with her.
And we traveled with her.
In different departments, first, we got on the train on, on a Sunday morning to go to, to Switzerland.
Well, you don't go straight to Switzerland, you know, especially in the middle of the war.
So we went-- the first hurdle, of course, was the border into France.
You had to get through France.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother had made some money.
She had about $100 or maybe $200.
So we were very much afraid.
We didn't want to go without anything.
But she wanted to-- we didn't know what to do with it.
So we had a friend.
That was an interesting thing.
Everybody put it in shoes.
And the shoes were the first thing they put-- the Germans looked at.
So we had a friend who came from Eastern Europe originally.
And he said, ah, don't worry.
And he took a rye bread.
And he cut it in slices.
And he put the, the dollar bills in between.
And very fine with the thread, he sewed two slices together.
And you could not see it.
And who would look in a bread, you know?
So we, we got this-- my mother kept this $100 all through the war.
And we made it over the border somehow.
And we came to the first city where we-- French city where we had to change.
And we had to stay overnight.
And when we got out of the station, out of the train, to get out of the station, there was SS looking at everybody then because they knew that people took this route to go to Switzerland.
But mother looked them right straight in the eye.
And we walked straight by them.
And we got out.
And we stayed overnight in a little hotel.
And we met up with this lady again.
The next morning we had breakfast at a little cafe.
And we were all sitting at the round table, you know, the three children.
And we were, again, four, six people by then.
And here comes the SS in again with big chains.
They had these-- the police had these big, white chains on their helmets.
And the children were really too little to understand.
But they were quiet.
And we didn't say a word.
And I think everybody's heart just stood still, you know.
And they said, well, I don't think there are any Jews here.
And they left.
